home.aboutText:
Welding engineer (IWE) with long-term hands-on experience implementing and optimizing MIG/MAG, TIG, and MMA — from welding parameters to quality control.

I train teams and support industrial projects. Author of a book on MAG/MIG welding. Full biography, diplomas, and certificates are on a dedicated page.

about.title: About Me
about.achievement1: Author of the practical guide “MAG/MIG welding” (2024).
about.achievement2: Welding engineer (IWE): implementation and auditing of MIG/MAG, TIG, and MMA processes.
about.achievement3: Experience supporting industrial production and training welders in industry and training centres.
about.achievement4: Expertise in shielding gas selection and welding equipment.
about.bachelorSummary: Higher education: bachelor’s degree from RTU.
about.masterSummary: RTU master’s degree — advanced engineering preparation.
about.mma_magSummary: Qualification in manual metal arc and semi-automatic welding (MMA/MAG).
about.fallbackBio: Welding engineer with extensive experience in MIG/MAG, TIG, and MMA processes. Author of the book "MAG/MIG welding". Expert in shielding gases and welding equipment.
about.fallbackEducation: Bachelor and Master degrees from RTU (Riga Technical University).
about.fallbackQualifications: Certified in MMA/MAG and TIG welding. Welding instructor qualification.

experience.title: Professional Experience
experience.projectsIntro: Consulting on parameters and gases, on-site process rollout, training programmes and certification support — from pilot runs to stable output.
experience.resultsIntro: Fewer rejects and rework through aligned parameters and control; clear recommendations for engineering and production; consistent skill levels after training.
experience.testimonial1: “Typical engagement: on-site engineering support with documented recommendations; priority is reproducible weld quality and process economics.”
experience.testimonialAttribution: Common client requests: consulting, implementation, training.
experience.casesIntro: Short, practical examples — open a card for context, typical results, and a link to follow up (blog, tools, or contact).
experience.case1Title: Shielding gas and MAG parameters for a fabrication shop
experience.case1Summary: Aligned gas choice, flow, and wire settings with real joint access — fewer pores and less rework.
experience.case1Detail: Started from current WPS drafts and shop constraints, then validated settings on sample plates and production trials. Documented the final parameter window so welders could reproduce results across shifts.
experience.case2Title: TIG aluminum — heat input and bead control
experience.case2Summary: Stabilized appearance and mechanical consistency by tuning balance, travel, and filler rhythm for thin sections.
experience.case2Detail: Mapped common defect triggers (contamination, overheating) to measurable adjustments; trained the team on torch angle and filler timing checkpoints.
experience.case3Summary: Built a pragmatic training loop and traceability pack for internal audits and customer questionnaires.
experience.case3Detail: Combined hands-on demos with short checklists for supervisors: what to verify before releasing a batch, and how to capture parameters consistently.

heat-input.lead: Estimates heat input (kJ/mm) from voltage, current and travel speed. For welding engineers and technicians comparing WPS values and thermal load.
heat-input.exampleCaption: Two different heat-input levels: higher Q usually means more energy per unit length in the weld.

gas-flow.lead: Estimates gas consumption over welding time and relates it to cylinder volume. Helpful when planning jobs and cylinder stock.
gas-flow.exampleCaption: Flow rate multiplied by time gives volume; compare with cylinder capacity for run time.

shielding-gas.lead: Suggests a reasonable shielding gas flow range from wire size, material and process. Use as a starting point before fine-tuning with tests.
shielding-gas.exampleCaption: Min–max band with a typical setpoint; adjust for nozzle, draft and joint access.

gas-cutting.lead: Oxygen cutting: indicative O₂ pressure and fuel flow from plate thickness (and optional cutting speed). Always verify with equipment manuals and trials.
gas-cutting.exampleCaption: Thicker plate generally needs higher oxygen pressure; values are indicative.

welding-cost.lead: Rough cost split between filler wire and shielding gas from prices, deposition rate and time. For estimators and workshop planning.
welding-cost.exampleCaption: Illustrative share of wire vs gas; your actual split depends on parameters and prices.

welding-parameters.lead: Starting-point current, voltage and travel speed from plate thickness, joint and wire diameter. Confirm on samples and your WPS.
welding-parameters.exampleCaption: Example parameter set (I, U, travel speed) for a typical MAG fillet — not a substitute for procedure qualification.

hints.voltage: Arc or load voltage used in the heat-input formula (check whether your WPS uses measured arc voltage).
hints.current: Welding current in amperes; must match the process and wire size you use.
hints.travelSpeed: Torch travel speed along the joint; must be greater than zero. Use consistent units (mm/min here).

hints.flowRate: Shielding gas flow at the torch (L/min), as set on the flowmeter or regulator.
hints.weldingTime: Total arc time in minutes for the calculation period (not necessarily clock time).
hints.cylinderVolume: Gas volume of the full cylinder in litres (often stated on the cylinder or data sheet).

hints.wireDiameter: Filler wire diameter; larger wires often allow a slightly wider flow window.
hints.material: Base material group (steel, stainless, aluminium) affects recommended shielding and flow.
hints.process: MIG/MAG or TIG — different torch and shielding behaviour.

hints.plateThickness: Thickness of the plate to cut; drives oxygen pressure and cutting setup.
hints.gasType: Fuel gas type (e.g. acetylene or propane) affects fuel flow recommendations.
hints.cuttingSpeed: Optional: actual cutting speed to refine estimates; leave empty if unknown.

hints.wirePrice: Purchase price per kilogram of filler wire.
hints.gasPrice: Price per full cylinder of shielding gas (same units as in your quote).
hints.cylinderVolume: Gas content of one cylinder in litres, for consumption and cost conversion.
hints.depositionRate: Mass of metal deposited per hour for your parameters (from datasheets or measurements).
hints.weldingTime: Arc hours for the job or batch being estimated.

hints.plateThickness: Main plate thickness in the joint; primary input for suggested heat.
hints.jointType: Butt, fillet, lap or corner — influences recommended parameters.
hints.wireDiameter: Filler wire diameter used in the process.
